narration.default: Today you'll write a tiny shopping bill. The kid says how many items they bought, then types the price of each one — and Python adds them up. You'll meet two new tools at once: the for loop with range, and a running total. By the end you'll have a working cashier program.

steps.title: Print a title and ask for the count

narration.default:
Type:

print("Smart Shopping Bill")

n = int(input("How many items? "))

The variable n holds the number of items. We wrap input in int because we'll loop that many times — and you can't loop a string. Notice we picked a short name, n — that's a common Python convention for "count" variables.

Type:

total = 0

This is the running total — empty at the start. Every loop iteration will add the new price into total. The pattern "start at zero, add as you go" shows up everywhere — counting, scoring, summing — so it's worth noticing.

Type:

for i in range(n):
    price = int(input("Enter price: "))
    total = total + price

for and range are both standard Python. range(n) makes a sequence of numbers 0, 1, 2 … up to n-1 — exactly n numbers. for i in range(n) means "do the indented block n times". Each loop the kid types a price, and total = total + price adds it on top of whatever total already had.

steps.title: Print the total and apply a discount

narration.default:
Back to no indent — the discount logic runs ONCE, after the loop has added every price:

print("Total Bill:", total)

if total > 100:
    print("Discount applied!")
    total = total - 10

print("Final Amount:", total)

Notice print can take more than one thing — it'll glue them with a space. The if checks the running total and knocks 10 off if it's big enough.

Click Run. Type 3 items. Enter prices 20, 30, 40. The terminal prints Total Bill: 90 — under 100, no discount, Final Amount: 90. Your for loop ran exactly three times because range(3) makes 0, 1, 2 — three values.
Run again. This time type 4 items. Enter 50, 50, 50, 50. Total: 200. Now the if catches it — Discount applied!, and Final Amount: 190. Same code, two different stories, depending on the prices.

Three new ideas in one tutorial: for loops, range, and running totals. With these you can score a quiz, count up dance moves, build a virtual piggy bank, or sum up steps in a fitness app. Next tutorial we'll add a quantity per item — what if the kid buys 3 of the same thing?
